summary: Cantrill highlights two approaches to getting DTrace on Linux: porting DTrace directly, or running Linux binaries under Solaris using the BrandZ framework. He points to Adam Leventhal's work demonstrating the latter approach, where DTrace was used to diagnose a serious Linux-specific performance problem in the 'top' utility. The post showcases how Solaris's compatibility layer enables powerful debugging tools to be applied to Linux applications.
key_points: DTrace can be brought to other operating systems either through porting or by running those systems under Solaris
BrandZ framework enables Linux binaries to run under Solaris
DTrace was used to find a serious performance problem in the Linux 'top' utility
money_quotes: There are two ways to get DTrace for another operating system: you can try porting DTrace to the other system, or you can use the new BrandZ framework to get that other system running under Solaris.
key_insight: Rather than waiting for tools to be ported to your platform, sometimes it's more practical to run your platform under a system that already has those tools.
